title: Metallic bonding — why metals conduct, bend and shine
examTip: Every property of a metal traces back to the sea of delocalised electrons. Conductivity? Electrons move freely. Malleability? Ions slide while the electron sea holds it together. High melting point? Strong attraction between ions and electron sea.
introTips: Metals are the workhorses of the material world — we use them for wires, bridges, coins, cans and aircraft. But why?
The answer is in the bonding: a unique structure where electrons are shared across the entire piece of metal.
Once you understand metallic bonding, every property — conductivity, malleability, shininess, high melting point — makes complete sense.
intro: Why can you bend a copper pipe but you'd shatter a piece of rock salt if you tried the same thing? Why does metal feel cold when you touch it, but wood at the same temperature doesn't? Why is copper used in electrical wiring but not diamond? All of these questions have the same answer: metallic bonding. Metals have a completely unique type of bonding — a 'sea' of electrons that flows freely through the whole material — and this one structural feature explains almost every property that makes metals so useful.

keyPoints.heading: What metallic bonding is
keyPoints.content: In a metal, each atom loses its outer electrons and becomes a positive ion (cation). These outer electrons don't stay attached to any one atom — they are free to move throughout the entire metal, forming a 'sea of delocalised electrons'. The metal is held together by the strong electrostatic attraction between the positively charged metal ions (which are arranged in a regular lattice) and the negatively charged sea of delocalised electrons surrounding them. This is metallic bonding. It is a type of giant structure — the bonding extends throughout the whole piece of metal. All metals have metallic bonding, from soft sodium to hard tungsten. The more outer electrons a metal atom releases, the more delocalised electrons in the sea, and the stronger the metallic bonding — this is why aluminium (3 outer electrons) has a higher melting point than sodium (1 outer electron).
writeBullets: Metal atoms lose outer electrons → become positive ions (cations)
Released electrons form a 'sea of delocalised electrons' moving freely
Metal held together by electrostatic attraction: positive ions ↔ electron sea
Giant structure — bonding extends throughout the whole piece of metal
All metals have metallic bonding
More outer electrons released = more delocalised electrons = stronger bonding

keyTerms.def: The electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a sea of delocalised electrons
Electrons not attached to any single atom; free to move throughout the metal lattice
A structure where bonding extends throughout the entire substance (as opposed to individual molecules)

keyPoints.memTip: Think of the metal as a ball-pit: the balls are positive ions sitting in a fixed lattice; the air flowing around them is the electron sea. The attraction between the balls and the 'air' holds everything together.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say 'the electrons are shared' — be precise: they are DELOCALISED, meaning they belong to no single atom and can move freely through the whole structure.

keyPoints.heading: Properties explained — electrical and thermal conductivity
keyPoints.content: Electrical conductivity: when a voltage is applied across a metal, the delocalised electrons move in one direction, carrying electrical charge through the metal — this is an electric current. Metals are good electrical conductors because of these free-moving electrons. Non-metals (except graphite) don't have delocalised electrons, so they can't conduct electricity. This is why copper is used in electrical wires — it's an excellent conductor and is relatively cheap. Thermal conductivity: when one end of a metal is heated, the delocalised electrons gain kinetic energy and move rapidly through the metal, transferring this energy to cooler parts. The vibrations of the positive ions in the lattice also help transfer energy. Metals are good thermal conductors because of both of these mechanisms. This is why metals feel cold to touch — they rapidly conduct heat away from your skin — and why aluminium and copper are used in saucepan bases.
writeBullets: Electrical conductivity: delocalised electrons move freely under voltage → carry charge
All metals conduct electricity; graphite is the only non-metal that does
Copper used for wiring: excellent conductor, relatively cheap, ductile
Thermal conductivity: delocalised electrons gain KE and transfer heat rapidly
Ion lattice vibrations also transfer thermal energy
Metals feel cold to touch: they rapidly conduct heat away from your skin

keyTerms.def: A substance that allows electrical current to flow through it; metals conduct due to delocalised electrons
A substance that transfers heat energy readily; metals conduct thermally via delocalised electrons and ion vibrations

keyPoints.memTip: CONDUCTIVITY (both electrical and thermal) = DELOCALISED ELECTRONS can MOVE. No delocalised electrons = no conductivity. Simple.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The 'why does metal feel cold?' question is a great real-life hook. Wooden and metal objects in the same room are at the same temperature — but the metal conducts heat away from your skin faster, so it FEELS colder.

keyPoints.heading: Properties explained — melting point, malleability and lustre
keyPoints.content: High melting and boiling points: to melt a metal, you must overcome the strong electrostatic attractions between the positive ions and the electron sea. This requires a large amount of energy — hence metals generally have high melting points. Tungsten melts at 3422°C; iron at 1538°C. (Mercury is an exception — it is liquid at room temperature.) Malleability and ductility: in a metal, the layers of positive ions can slide over each other when force is applied — the electron sea simply flows with them, maintaining the bonding throughout. This means metals don't crack or shatter — they deform. This makes metals malleable (can be hammered into shape) and ductile (can be drawn into wires). Contrast this with ionic compounds — when you strike ionic crystals, like charges end up next to each other and repel, causing the crystal to shatter. Lustre (shininess): the delocalised electrons on the surface of a metal reflect light waves — this is why polished metals appear shiny.
writeBullets: High melting point: strong electrostatic forces need lots of energy to overcome
Malleability: layers of ions slide over each other; electron sea flows with them
Metals don't shatter — unlike ionic crystals where sliding brings like charges together
Ductility: metals can be drawn into wires because layers slide easily
Lustre: delocalised electrons on surface reflect light → shiny appearance
Mercury is exceptional — liquid metal at room temperature (very weak metallic bonding)

keyTerms.def: Able to be hammered or pressed into shape without breaking (e.g. gold, copper)
Able to be drawn out into a wire without breaking (e.g. copper, aluminium)
A shiny appearance; caused by delocalised electrons on the metal surface reflecting light

keyPoints.memTip: MALLEABLE = can be moulded. DUCTILE = can be drawn into wires. Both are possible because the electron SEA FLOWS while layers slide — the bonding never actually breaks.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The contrast with ionic bonding (which shatters) is a high-value comparison for exams. Make sure Mabel understands WHY ionic compounds shatter: like charges align and repel when layers shift.

keyPoints.heading: Alloys — improving on pure metals
keyPoints.content: Pure metals have a regular lattice of identical-sized ions. These layers slide over each other very easily, which makes pure metals quite soft. Alloys are mixtures of a metal with one or more other elements (usually other metals, but sometimes non-metals like carbon). The key effect: the added atoms are a different size, so they distort the regular lattice. This prevents the layers from sliding as easily — the alloy is therefore harder and stronger than the pure metal. The composition of an alloy can be controlled to tune its properties. Important alloys: Steel (iron + 0.1–2% carbon — much stronger and harder than pure iron; stainless steel also contains chromium and nickel, making it rust-resistant). Bronze (copper + tin — harder than copper; used for tools, medals, sculptures). Brass (copper + zinc — harder than copper, good acoustic properties; used in musical instruments, door fittings). Solder (tin + lead — low melting point; used for joining electrical components). Duralumin (aluminium + 4% copper — lightweight and strong; used in aircraft construction).
writeBullets: Pure metals: regular lattice of same-sized ions → layers slide easily → soft
Alloys: different-sized atoms distort the lattice → layers can't slide easily → harder
Steel: iron + carbon → stronger than iron; stainless steel also has Cr and Ni
Bronze: copper + tin; Brass: copper + zinc (musical instruments)
Solder: tin + lead, low melting point (joining circuits)
Duralumin: aluminium + copper, lightweight + strong (aircraft)

keyTerms.def: A mixture of a metal with one or more other elements, designed to have improved properties
An alloy of iron and carbon (0.1–2%); stronger and harder than pure iron
An alloy of aluminium and copper; lightweight and strong, used in aircraft

keyPoints.memTip: Pure metal = all same-sized balls in a tray — slides easily. Alloy = add some bigger or smaller balls — they get stuck. That's why alloys are harder.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam questions often ask 'why is steel harder than iron?' — the answer must mention: different-sized atoms, distorted lattice, layers can't slide as easily.

tables.title: Pure metals vs alloys — comparing properties
rows: Harder — layers cannot slide as easily
Usually similar to or slightly lower than pure metal

0.question: Which feature of metallic bonding explains why metals are good electrical conductors?
options: Delocalised electrons that can move freely throughout the metal
Positive ions arranged in a regular lattice
Strong electrostatic attraction between ions and electrons
High melting points requiring lots of energy to melt
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right! The delocalised electrons are not attached to any single atom — when a voltage is applied, they can move freely in one direction, creating an electric current. This is why all metals conduct electricity.
0.wrongFeedback: Electrical conductivity requires charge carriers that can move. In metals, the delocalised electrons are free to move through the whole lattice — they carry the electrical charge when a voltage is applied. The lattice structure and strong bonds explain other properties (melting point, malleability), not conductivity.

3.question: Why is steel harder than pure iron?
options: Carbon atoms distort the iron lattice, preventing layers from sliding easily
Steel has more delocalised electrons than iron
Steel has a higher melting point than iron
Iron atoms in steel are larger, creating a stronger lattice
3.correctFeedback: Perfect explanation! Carbon atoms are a different size from iron atoms. When they're present in the lattice, they disrupt the regular pattern and prevent the layers of iron ions from sliding over each other — this is what makes steel harder and stronger than pure iron.
3.wrongFeedback: The key is the size difference. Carbon atoms are a different size from iron atoms, so they distort the regular iron lattice. This makes it harder for layers to slide over each other — which is why steel is harder than pure iron. The number of delocalised electrons doesn't explain hardness.

commonMistakes: Saying that metals conduct because they have 'free electrons' without explaining that these electrons are delocalised — the word 'delocalised' is key in AQA mark schemes.
Thinking alloys have higher melting points than pure metals — they often don't (solder has a deliberately low melting point). The key improvement is hardness/strength, not melting point.
Confusing malleable and ductile — malleable = can be hammered into flat shapes; ductile = can be drawn into wires. Both happen because layers of ions slide in the electron sea.
Explaining conductivity by saying 'electrons move' without saying WHY they can move — always add 'because they are delocalised and not attached to any single atom'.
Forgetting that more outer electrons = stronger metallic bonding — aluminium (3 outer e) has a higher melting point than sodium (1 outer e) because it contributes more electrons to the sea.
examTips: In any question about metallic properties, structure your answer around the sea of delocalised electrons. Conductivity, malleability, thermal transfer — they all link back to this one feature.
If asked why an alloy is harder than a pure metal, always include: 'different-sized atoms distort the regular lattice, preventing layers of ions from sliding over each other'.
For 6-mark extended questions comparing metals and alloys, structure your answer: pure metal structure → layers slide → soft → alloy structure → layers can't slide → harder. Include examples.
When asked why metals have high melting points, say: 'strong electrostatic attraction between the positive ions and the sea of delocalised electrons requires a large amount of energy to overcome'.
revisionCardBullets: Metallic bonding: positive ions in lattice surrounded by sea of delocalised electrons
Held together by electrostatic attraction between positive ions and electron sea
Electrical conductivity: delocalised electrons move freely to carry charge
Thermal conductivity: electrons transfer heat; also ion lattice vibrations
High melting points: strong electrostatic forces require lots of energy to break
Malleable: layers of ions slide over each other; electron sea flows with them
Ductile: can be drawn into wires (layers slide; bonding maintained)
Lustre: delocalised electrons on surface reflect light
Pure metals: regular lattice, identical-sized ions → layers slide → soft
Alloys: different-sized atoms distort lattice → layers can't slide → harder
Steel (Fe + C), bronze (Cu + Sn), brass (Cu + Zn), duralumin (Al + Cu)
More outer electrons released = stronger metallic bonding = higher melting point
